Brand Style Guide — v1.0

Logo system, color tokens, typography, moodboard direction, and component library for a narrative strategy consultancy that earns its authority.

Protagonist Ink LLC Ethic Serif · Satoshi · Custom Script 2025 — v1.0
  • What We Believe
    • Philosophy
  • Identity & Design
    • Logo
    • Colors
    • Type
    • Editorial
    • Moodboard
    • UI Elements
  • Copy & Voice
    • Voice
  • Code Ref
    • Code Ref

00 — Brand Philosophy

The Central Tension

Humanity
and
System.

The hero's journey endures because it maps transformation. Ordinary world. Disruption. Resistance. Threshold. Ordeal. Return. It's not decorative narrative. It's structure that holds under pressure.

That's the thing about story: it looks like humanity, but it runs on structure. The two aren't opposites. They need each other. Pull out the structure and it's just feeling. Pull out the human experience and it's just a framework. Neither one moves anyone.

That's what Protagonist Ink does. We find the narrative architecture underneath an organization's work — the structure that's already there, often invisible to the people inside it — and we build the language that makes it visible to everyone else. It's not about making things sound better. It's about making them legible.

"The grid makes the claim. The image is why it matters."

Design Principle

"The site pulls from the designed world. The photography is cinematic and human. The brand lives in the tension between the two."

The Central Argument

The System

Architecture.
Framework.
Structure.

Story has always had structure. Three acts. The hero's journey. Rising action, crisis, resolution. These aren't constraints — they're the reason story works. The system is what makes emotional truth transmissible.

For Protagonist Ink, the designed world sets the frame. The grid, the type scale, the red annotation — before the copy, they show how the work is organized.

Type as Architecture The Red Pen Editorial Grid Sketch Lines

The Humanity

Presence.
Moment.
Consequence.

But structure without a person inside it is just architecture. The hero's journey matters because there's a hero. Someone who loses something, risks something, changes. That's the half no framework can fake.

The photography does this work visually. A performer mid-breath. Hands on a draft at midnight. Someone staring at a wall of ideas. These images don't illustrate the copy — they carry the stakes of it.

Cinematic Photo Intimate Distance The Break Real Stakes

How It Works Together

The site is
the system.
The work is
the proof.

The site builds from the designed world. Editorial grids, type at scale, structural confidence. The Rust marks are the red pen. The grid is how rigorous thinking looks when it's honest about being rigorous.

The photography is cinematic and human — real people, real stakes, mid-act. It doesn't sit inside the layout. It breaks the grid on purpose. The grid shows the system. The image brings the human stakes back into frame.

That's the brand. Not humanity or system. The tension between them, held without resolving it.

01 — Logo System

Nav Lockup Rule: Primary navbar logo width is fixed at 250px (desktop) and 180px (mobile).

Wordmark — Ink on Light Editorial surfaces, letterhead, light UI contexts
Wordmark — White on Black Dark hero surfaces, video overlays, full-bleed black
Wordmark — White on Ink Primary dark brand surface, feature sections
Wordmark — White on Deep Indigo Arts sector, cultural sector, thought leadership contexts

Construction

The logo is a lockup — not reproducible in type. "Protagonist" is set in Ethic Serif. "Ink" is a custom hand-drawn script. Never attempt to recreate or recompose it.

Clear Space

Minimum clear space = the cap-height of the "P" on all four sides. Never crowd the wordmark against edges, imagery, or other type.

Minimum Size

Digital: 160px wide minimum. Print: 1.5 inches wide minimum. Below these sizes, use the symbol mark only.

Never

Don't recolor, stretch, rotate, or add effects. Don't place on busy photographic backgrounds. Don't use on Rust — it's an accent color, not a logo surface.

01b — Symbol Mark

The quill-and-pen symbol functions as a standalone mark for social avatars, favicons, stamps, and any context where the full wordmark is too wide. It reads simultaneously as an "I" for Ink and a quill — craft as identity. The feathering in the center of the stroke is the actual texture of the original handwriting, which is what makes it feel alive rather than constructed.

Black on Light
White on Ink
Gray on Ink — textural variant
Social / Avatar

02 — Color Tokens

#2C2C2C

Ink

alias: charcoal

Primary. Deep charcoal. The default voice. Strategic confidence without loudness. Used for body text, headings, and primary UI.

#FAFAFA

Warm White

alias: paper

Background default. Editorial breathing room — the page beneath the prose. Slightly warm to avoid clinical sterility.

#C83C2F

Rust

alias: red

Accent. CTAs, highlights, editorial punctuation. The red pen. Use sparingly — when it appears, it means something.

#2E2B5F

Deep Indigo

alias: indigo

Secondary accent. Depth, intelligence, arts world credibility. Currently in tokens but not deployed in site Tailwind classes — reserve for arts sector contexts.

#9B9EA4

Cool Gray

alias: coolgray

Supporting. Captions, secondary text, structural elements, metadata. The quiet infrastructure of the page.

#0A0A0A

True Black

alias: trueblack

Maximum contrast. Hero sections, dark surfaces, cinema moments. Footer background. Distinct from Ink — reserved for full-bleed theatrical use.

Opacity System — Ink

ink/10

ink/20

ink/30

ink/40

ink/50

ink/70

ink/80

ink/90

Opacity System — Rust

rust/12

rust/20

rust/40

rust/50

rust/70

rust/100

Note: The site uses selection:bg-rust / selection:text-warmwhite — text selection is on-brand. Keep this in any new web contexts.

03 — Typography

Cormorant Garamond — Display & Headers  ·  wt 300–600 · roman + italic

Hero112px / wt 300
lh 0.85 · ls -0.03em

Narrative

Hero Italic112px / wt 300
italic · lh 0.85

Architecture

Display88px / wt 400
lh 0.9 · ls -0.02em

The Story Holds

H164px / wt 400
lh 1.0

Every Brand Has a Plot

H248px / wt 400
lh 1.05

What's the Through-Line?

H336px / wt 500
lh 1.1

The Protagonist's Journey

H4 Italic28px / wt 500
italic · lh 1.2

Conflict, Stakes, Resolution

Satoshi — Body & Functional  ·  wt 300–700  ·  CSS var: --font-satoshi

Subhead22px / wt 300
lh 1.4 · ls 0.01em

We work with organizations that have something real to say.

Body Lg18px / wt 400
lh 1.65

Strategy without story is just a spreadsheet.

Body16px / wt 400
lh 1.7 · max 65ch

The best brands don't describe what they do — they embody how it feels to believe in something. Protagonist Ink helps organizations find that story and build everything around it.

Body Sm14px / wt 400
color: coolgray

Narrative strategy, brand voice, organizational storytelling, editorial positioning.

Caption12px / wt 400
ls 0.02em

Image: The working draft. Los Angeles, 2024.

Label11px / wt 500
uppercase · ls 0.2em

Narrative Strategy

Nav10–11px / wt 500
uppercase · ls 0.15em

Work  ·  About  ·  Journal  ·  Story Health Check

CTA12px / wt 500
uppercase · ls 0.12em
color: rust

Start the Conversation →

Micro9–10px / wt 500
uppercase · ls 0.2em

Fig. 01  ·  Patrick Kirkland  ·  Founder

04 — Editorial Color Combinations

Hero / Campaign
True Black Surface

The story
is the strategy.

Maximum authority. For hero moments, campaign headers, and brand statements that need to land with full weight.

Primary Dark
Ink Surface

Write the brief
before the deck.

Workhorse dark surface. Feature sections, service callouts, pullout moments within lighter pages.

Editorial Default
Warm White Surface

Every brand
has a plot.

Long-form editorial default. About pages, journal, case studies. The primary reading surface.

Arts / Depth
Deep Indigo Surface

Intelligence
earns trust.

Reserve for arts sector clients, cultural work, and thought leadership content with strong intellectual positioning.

05 — Brand Moodboard

The craft
is the
credential.

Protagonist Ink lives where creative rigor meets strategic clarity. The visual world draws from cinematic documentary — raw materials, focused makers, the weight of real work in progress.

Not polished stock photography. Not startup gradients. The aesthetic of a serious literary magazine crossed with a film treatment — editorial authority that doesn't announce itself.

The hero visual motif is the typewriter blueprint — technical drawing meets writing instrument. Craft as infrastructure.

00

Foundation Visual

The Blueprint
Hero Motif

Technical drawing of writing instruments. The typewriter schematic. Craft as infrastructure — the machine behind the words.

01

Direction One

The Stage
Photography

Makers at work. Performers in moment. Collaborators building something real. High contrast. Intimate distance. B&W preferred.

02

Direction Two

The Draft
Type as Design

Typography as architecture. Editorial layouts that command the page. Words as the primary visual element. Cinematic copy treatments.

Ink

The default voice. Not pure black — there's warmth in the charcoal. The color of conviction, not performance. It reads with authority without aggression.

Rust

The editorial mark. The red pen. The annotation. Used sparingly — when it appears, it means something. The one color that acts, while others describe.

Deep Indigo

The depth beneath the surface. Arts world credibility. The color of late-night thinking, Carnegie Hall programs, and ideas that need the right room to land.

06 — UI Elements

Buttons

Tags & Labels

Narrative Strategy New Arts Case Study Brand Voice 1 Week

Pull Quote

"The strategy only works if the story lands."

Stats

20+

Years in the room

Now

Work in progress

07 — Voice & Tone

The voice is
the credential.

Not the agency voice. Not the thought leader voice. The voice of someone who has been in the room, done the work, and can translate narrative craft into commercial outcomes — without announcing any of it.

"You don't have a messaging problem. You have a narrative architecture problem. That's the difference between polishing words and building the engine underneath them."

Signature Cadence

Names the false assumption → reframes it → defines the practical consequence.

Canonical Voice Block (Mirror Verbatim)

Identity

Screenwriter meets strategist.

Register

Founder to founder. Never vendor to client.

Position

Most clients do not have a messaging problem. They have a narrative architecture problem.

Guardrail

If it sounds like content, rewrite it until it sounds like someone who has done the work.

Identity

Screenwriter
meets strategist.

Confident, not performative. Precise, not ornate. Intelligent, without announcing intelligence.

Tone

Cinematic,
editorial, sharp.

Authority is quiet. Wit is dry and occasional. Claims are concrete and defensible.

Register

Founder
to founder.

Never vendor-to-client. Assume the reader can handle nuance. Write to intelligence, not around it.

Position

Narrative
architecture.

Most clients don't have a messaging problem. They have a narrative architecture problem. Hold that line.

Voice Principles

→

Lead with insight, not with a hook. Let it breathe one beat before explaining it.

→

Favor short declarative sentences that carry meaning.

→

Use specific language over broad claims.

→

Show business effect, not just language effect.

→

Challenge gently but directly.

→

End by opening a door, not issuing a command.

What to Avoid

✕

"We help brands tell their story" language.

✕

Listicle cadence and framework theater.

✕

Hype, hustle, or motivational filler.

✕

Therapy-speak and jargon padding.

✕

Hedging that blunts the point.

✕

Buzzword stacks without a real claim underneath.

Channel Guide

Website
Clear strategic authority. Claims sharp and tied to outcomes. No inflated language. Prioritize positioning clarity over cleverness.
LinkedIn
Lead with an earned insight from practice. No engagement hooks, no performative vulnerability. One idea per post, developed with concrete stakes.
Sales / Proposals
Same voice, more operational. Increase specificity around process, scope, and business impact. Name what's included and what isn't.
Strategy Docs
Most direct mode. Fewer flourishes, more precision. Name tradeoffs, constraints, and consequences plainly.
Scripts / Narrative
Allow more cinematic rhythm, but preserve strategic clarity. Style cannot outrun point.

Writing Formula (Internal)

1

State the real problem in one line.

2

Reframe what the client thinks the problem is.

3

Name the structural intervention — narrative architecture.

4

Show the operational or business consequence.

5

Close with a next move, not a slogan.

Editing Filter (Pre-Publish)

1

Does this sound like a founder speaking to another founder?

2

Is every major claim concrete and defensible?

3

Did we remove lines that sound like agency brochure copy?

4

Does the ending open a useful next step?

5

If this were unattributed, would it still unmistakably sound like Protagonist Ink?

Guardrail

If it sounds like "content," rewrite it until it sounds like someone who has done the work.

08 — Codebase Reference

Actual Tailwind class conventions used in the live site. Use these when building new components to stay consistent with the existing token system.

Color Token Classes

/* Backgrounds */ bg-ink /* #2C2C2C */ bg-rust /* #C83C2F */ bg-trueblack /* #0A0A0A */ bg-[#FAFAFA] /* warmwhite */ /* Text */ text-ink /* primary */ text-rust /* accent/CTA */ text-warmwhite /* on-dark */ /* Borders */ border-ink/10 /* dividers */ border-rust/40 /* accented border */ border-rust/70 /* strong accent */

Opacity Variants In Use

/* Ink opacity scale */ text-ink/15 text-ink/20 text-ink/25 text-ink/30 text-ink/35 text-ink/40 text-ink/50 text-ink/70 text-ink/80 text-ink/90 /* Rust opacity */ border-rust/40 border-rust/70 bg-rust/[0.12] /* Selection color */ selection:bg-rust selection:text-warmwhite

Font Stack

/* CSS Variable */ --font-satoshi: "Satoshi", "Avenir Next", "Segoe UI", system-ui, sans-serif; /* Cormorant loaded weights */ 300, 400, 500, 600 roman + italic for all /* Satoshi loaded weights */ 300, 400, 500, 700

Type Size Conventions

/* Micro / UI labels */ text-[9px] /* fig labels */ text-[10px] /* micro labels */ text-[11px] /* nav items */ text-[12px] /* captions */ text-[13px] /* small body */ text-[15px] /* body */ /* Responsive display */ md:text-4xl /* section heads */ md:text-7xl /* hero display */ lg:text-[5.5rem] lg:text-[2.2rem]

Narrative Strategy · Brand Voice · Organizational Storytelling

Style Guide v1.0 — 2025
